speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two of the interview with Ellen Picker. Ellen, when you were in Poland were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you film too?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a school nearby. And it was actually half taught in German and half in Polish. In the morning, it was this and the afternoon was the other. So I went to the German-speaking school. But still the Polish subjects was the most important, like history and things like that, of course. They spoke all two languages in this town, near the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot to do with the Jewish community in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had lots of Jewish friends, lots and had a wonderful time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that community for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just young people-- we went swimming together. And you know, it was also lovely in a sense. It was really beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't-- I don't think they belonged to any other organizations. I know of one boy who was meant to-- he was Olympic material in diving. His name was [? Jacob ?] [? Prada. ?] That's the only name I remember. And we lived with Jewish people. And they had a [INAUDIBLE], sort of. And one day, we came back from holidays. We locked our [? bottles. ?] The whole jewelery was gone. But we didn't say a word. Because I wanted first to make sure of it. And I had a friend who was a jeweler on the same street. And I went to him. And he said, you draw the pieces here. And he knew where they are. The husband had stolen them. And the, and the wife hit, hit him something terrible. And these people are called [? Raisha. ?] He stole our jeweleries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really very much. We, we must have been aware. Because we were anxious to, to know. But as a child, you know, you go with your youngsters. And you have fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what stage did your father leave you to go to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia was taken by, by Hitler. And so there were lots of Czechoslovakian migrants. And took-- we, we invited them for-- to eat. And [? we paid the lady. ?] It was [INAUDIBLE]. And she had about five visitors every so often, always the same, very nice people. And my father, as he was-- you ha-- in, in Europe when you go to a town, you have to register yourself within 24 hours. You know that. And he was in Czechoslovakia. One day, he got a letter-- this must be '39, beginning. It was pretty late in the day, before the war-- but before the war, a few, few months before the war. And he was invited to go as a transfer to London. It was a Czech-- it was a Czechoslovakian [INAUDIBLE]. So he took the opportunity. We thought it was fabulous. The, the, the permit was on the way any moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: The permit to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Australia, any moment. And he would be there and would wait for us there. And we never got the permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: We never got the permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did he leave you? What-- do you remember what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the exact date. I know that my mother broke down. And she told-- she told me later on that she knew she will never see him again. She broke down in that, at the, at the, at the station. And she waved. And it was terrible. I was-- I loved my hus-- my, my father so much. When I was a little girl, I always said, I'm going to marry you. [LAUGHS] I always said, I'm going to marry you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you and your mother alone when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were alone. And then we were taken, of course, at night. when the war broke out '41. I asked a communist friend, a Jewish boy-- lots of Jewish people out where we went, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living there, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. It was very near the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, so you left Poland when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that was still Poland. But it was really near Russia--

speaker: interviewer
text: Under--right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other half. And I had a very nice circle of friends-- very, very nice. And a very strange thing happened. We went to the post office. And we could speak very little Russia. But we spoke Russian to them. And we wanted to do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very nice young man approached us. Could he help us? I was friends with him then a long time. And you know who that was? It was my husband's cousin. Isn't it a coincidence? Unbelievable. I met his uncle-- unbelievable. So were you were living under Russian occupation then, were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, two years-- and it was very good. It was wonderful when the Russian army came into the city with-- on white horses. And it was like in a film, you know? Like, it was unbelievable. And we were so relieved, because the Ukrainians, Ukrainians are very-- Ukrainians [? and ?] Jews were very anti-Semitics.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were pogroms since the First World War. And people said the Jewish blood was flowing like a river. They cut off fingers off with rings and all that. Can you imagine that? But it was very Jewish then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- we were-- a lot of the Ukrainians who most probably prayed that Hitler would come, you know. And we, we prayed the opposite. But-- it was-- we lived quite well. I don't-- my mother must have done something, too. I don't know who paid us. We lived in a little house in, on a farm with another Jewish family. And he, he was, he did, he had always goose and put food into the you know, into the-- sorry. It was he wanted to fatten up geese. And he had a goat and a little child and no money. And we lived with him in the little house, one room each.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much contact with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, yes, all the time, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what sort of things was he telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew where he was working, and lovely letters, you know? I have the letters-- I have still letters I wrote to him and my mother wrote to him. We all kept it. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was living in London, trying to organize--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get you and your mother out, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but then, it was too late. When the war broke out, it was too late. And we were already in Russia. Yeah, when the war broke out, '41, I asked a communist Jewish friend. I said, what will happen to us now? He said, I don't know. But actually, we felt very safe because we had this-- I had the Jewish passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but they had already sent away lots of people. There was always 20, 30 people missing next morning, next morning. This always went at night. I remember when we came out once. And a woman fell around our neck, ah, you are still here. How wonderful, you know? They just sent everybody away-- Ukrainians too, unfortunately. So-- what did I want to say? As soon as the war broke out, they took us. At night, they came. Take your things and come along. This was typical as Russians-- always take your things and come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we already were prepared. I said to my mother many months before, you know, we have to have the best rucksacks we can afford so we can carry a lot. But we couldn't carry, because we had to walk 80 kilometers. So we couldn't carry. We just could, you know, leave it where it was. But we, we were sent at night to [INAUDIBLE] into the prison. We came to [INAUDIBLE] and prison was full. People-- all prisons were full in Ger-- in Russia. People-- like a prison cell like that, there would be at least 40 people in it, maybe 50. We lay on the floor without bedding on both sides. And when one person turns, the whole row had to turn. Because there was now-- we couldn't lie like that. It's always-- there was no room-- there were so, so many prisoners that they have.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in this prison And I remember one Jewish lady. I'll never forget her. She was clinging to her belongings, a la-- an elderly lady. She wouldn't have made it, in any case. And they gave us a herring and a bread and said, hold onto it. You won't get anything to eat for a long time. Little did we know that we had walked to the Russian border, 80 kilometers. We didn't know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was absolutely-- this, this was the worst experience. A few times long-- we have to walk quite often. And this lady wanted to take a thing she couldn't carry. And she didn't know, know where her husband is. And I mean, they didn't leave her behind. They shot the people who couldn't make it. It was terrible. In this prison-- in the first prison-- I don't know how long we were there. I lost so much weight that the skirt I was wearing, I had to do-- I put the pin in or whatever I had, at least a pleat like that, in no time. We were at the end, we were just skin and bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when we came to one camp, my mother and I, that was not a political camp. It was a camp where [? murder ?] wasn't-- better people, you know? They felt so sorry for us when they saw us in the, in the bath-- the bath was a little dish with water-- that they offered us food, more food and everything. Just explain to me how you were rounded up to take-- and taken to Russia. Were you living in a ghetto? Or were you marked as foreigners, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. [INAUDIBLE]. We lived in this little house. And at night, they came and took us, was a big transport. And the transport looked like that. We were in a, in a big car, not, not closed like a truck. And, and then they put the cloth over you so nobody could see you from outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the nationalities of most of the people that they took?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very many Jewish and very many Ukrainians-- and when we went to Russia in cattle trains, of course, when you hear the-- you heard the guns and all-- on the way to the, to, to the Russian border where we had to walk 80 kilometers, there were already lots of bodies laying, lying there. There were already lots of war going on. And-- but you know, we were machines. We just took one [INAUDIBLE]. It was-- we were completely-- how do you call it-- we were completely numb of any feelings the whole time in Russia. It was just a surviving business. And we were certain-- at the last camp where I met my husband, that was the best camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were families together. Before we went only-- we were only with women, of course. And then we came to this camp. There were families. And it was much better. People fell in love and you know? What sort of work did you do in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always on the fields, always-- things [? remember ?] Jews. The hands would bleed at night. And what do we do? We wash them with urine. This was the only thing you could do. They dried off then, you know? Next day, again, especially when the harvest was on. And it was cut by hand. And we had to roll it up into little things. And then put it like that. And this, this makes, makes your hands so bloody. We are not used to things like that, all day long. And it was threshed with, with a machine. Sometimes with a machine-- when the machines stopped, we had to thresh it by hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the accommodation like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only barracks, and, and boards-- no bedding, nothing. In the end, in the, in last camp, they gave us-- not in the beginning-- we got mattress covers made out of sacking. And they put a huge pile of straw in the yard. And we filled this, you know? And ever so often, they would put new straw. And we could refill it. Because it got very flat very soon. That was in the last camp, where I met my husband. And that was already much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like? How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you how we were treated. I came into one prison. We went into the yard. And we had to strip our clothes off completely. There were all soldiers. But they saw that every day, in any case. They wouldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had to go on a board. It was about that high. And a woman examined your vaginas, if you have something hidden in it-- as if you could. Maybe at another prison, we came from another prison. Would you believe that? It was really, we were so degraded in every respect. When we went on this long walk, they, they said, men, right, women left. But we sat there and did our business together. What can you do? What can you do? And the whole time you were in Russia, you were with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was sometimes-- they wanted to part us. And I was very courageous one day. They said, your mother will go in this camp because she was in a different grade. I was a heavy worker. And she was a light worker. And they wanted to part us, because there were no unions in, in, in, In prisons, no unions. We were all single people, because unions are too strong.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we made a scene there one day. When my mother had to go to a-- they took her to, to hospital. And she was away for a couple of months. And I always asked, how is my mother? And they were so horrid. Your mother is long dead. You don't need [? always ?] this old woman. She wasn't old. She was only in her early forties. And then one day, the door opened. And she came in. And we fell each other in the arm. And we cried and cried. And the whole 80 people cried and cried. And we were not allowed to do that, you know? This created a-- this created something, which was-- but they said to stop it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and you know, on the-- at the door there, was a, a big dish thing like that with two handles. That was our toilet, with a wooden, with a wooden thing like that on the top. And we had to beg to take it out when it was full. We only went to the toilet once a day. And the toilets were a row, a big-- a long row in, in the prisons, where you put your feet on. And the water was going all the time. There was nothing to sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember once when I was called to a, to a-- what do you, do you call that if you are asked? Interrogated, yes? First, my mother went out first. She stayed out in half an hour. And then when she came in, they took me out straight away. No, no, no time to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was there, he said-- and that was the first time in years I was sitting on a chair. And you have no idea what it meant. It was something wonderful. He made me sit down on the chair. And everything I said, but your mother said something completely different. And I said, it can't be. Because it wouldn't be true. She didn't say anything different. We never lied. We never lied-- ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of questions were they asking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What Germany-- how Germany-- they thought we are German spies. They thought we are German spies. I said, I have a J in the passport. That's nothing. That doesn't say a thing. It could-- it doesn't say a thing. Because they could've put a J onto anybody's pass. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time that you were in Russia, were you able to communicate with your father at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all-- we tried, but it's only the one letter that came through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this letter, I have it here, too. But it would take a long time to look for. Somebody said to my mother, I'll get a letter through for you. She knew that he was in [INAUDIBLE]. So he made the envelope and put half of it in Russian, half of it in English. And this letter arrived. And this letter was smuggled out of the camp. And it arrived. We had no paper, no pens, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother worked in the art studio. She wasn't a heavy worker. She worked in the art studio. She painted materials, you know? And so she, she had access to paper, a little bit of paper. And she had access to such things. And that was, that was [? thought-- ?] that was done in this, at the studio, this letter. And it arrived. My father got it. So he knew she was alive. He was asking about me, but-- So how did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was only asking where he is. She is searching for him, you know? She didn't know that he will get this letter into his hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the coincidence of your father getting the letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father worked in the department for, for immigrants. And I don't think he was paid, I don't know. Most probably, he was paid something. He had to live. And he must have-- he, he opened the letters-- the search letters. And he, of course, saw straight away my mother's handwriting, because she had special handwriting. He must, he must have seen it straight away. And that was the letter he wrote. [? He doesn't-- ?] she didn't mention me. She only was searching him. And then he wrote back to the camp that he, he's was so happy. He was very ill by then. My, my husband worked in the kitchen. And he brought the letter to the barrack. And when we saw the letter with his handwriting, we couldn't open it. We stood there and cried. We couldn't open the letter. We couldn't open the letter. We were absolutely finished. And the same day he died when we got the letter. The same day we got the letter, he died. He must have waited for that. He had wonderful friends in England. And we corresponded. This one lady who was a friend of his until she died, and she said she was with him when he died. He thought that she was my mother. He, he, he said to her her name. And oh, the poems. You'll see how much he suffered when you read his poems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to learn anything about how the war was going whilst you were in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had no news whatsoever. I don't, I don't-- I said to my husband, did we know when the war was finished? I can't remember. We would have been ecstatic. But he said, yes, we did. I can't remember it. I can't remember it. I mean, we would have been berserk. I can't remember it. Then they prepared in Russia a certain group of people of different nationalities. They fed them very well for a while. They dressed them better. We had terrible things to dress. I'll tell you about the garments we had on. And all of the sudden, we are released into freedom. And at the last minute, they took our names with-- on, on material, written on material. And the names came up in the in America and in the news [INAUDIBLE]. And it said that internment camps are dissolved. And we were still there, most of us. And then the knew from every, every name and every person where we were. It, it was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Russia, all told?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, six years-- always a prisoner, always a prisoner. Yes, the garments we got-- twice a year, we got garments-- winter garments and summer garments. There was a certain date, no matter how the weather was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was all clothing from dead soldiers. You could see sometimes holes from gunshots. And there was always dried blood somewhere in it, you know? And when we went-- once a week, we went for the bath. The bath consisted of a room with boards. And on the boards were little like, like, barrels-- little, little pieces of barrels with maybe a pint of water. And soap was as much as a little piece of chocolate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our clothes was taken to the, to the, to the stove, to the stove. It was very, very hot so the lice would die. We didn't have the lice in this, in this camp. But in, in, in the prisons, we looked for lice from morning till night. People had lice, lice in their eyelashes-- they pulled them out-- and, and pubic hair-- they pulled it out. It was terrible-- clothes lice and, and, and body lice. The only person who hadn't lice was my mother. She never had lice. She had such slippery hair, they couldn't get onto her. And then they, then they shaved your head when you had lice. It's the best thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of illness in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strangely enough, not so much. I had-- on the way to Russia, to first prison, this was [INAUDIBLE], the first prison was [INAUDIBLE], I had-- I think I had dysentery. I had blood in the stool. And, and, the, the-- if my mother wouldn't have been there, I wouldn't have made it. You didn't have any toilet. There was only a little, little round thing going out of the train-- no, no windows, nothing. We came into this train. There was still-- was still not clean from the animals who had been there before. And men died like flies. Women are much, much stronger. You wouldn't believe it. And my-- she pushed my insides back, you know, with her hands. And I don't know how she managed with us. We had nothing and all the rubbish. I don't know how she managed. Without her, I wouldn't have been there. I didn't care anymore. [? It was ?] [? the state ?] I didn't care anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about meeting your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to the internment camp, which we missed years before because there was a bad illness in it. And there was a quarantine. And so we were pushed from one prison to the other, from one camp to the other. And we came there two-- that was only two years. And we came straight away to the hospital, my mom and I. There was a doctor, a Viennese doctor. And his mother was a, was a, was a top nurse. She didn't have [INAUDIBLE]. But she was, was really good. And she, she was sort of the boss from the whole hospital. And when we lay in this room and we got our food, somebody said, they build-- we build our, our [INAUDIBLE] ourselves. And we did the bricks ourselves with, with burnt out [? coke ?] and, and earth. And we had to-- after the work, we had to, to, to carry two of those bricks into the camp. They weighed 12 kilos each. It was terrible. You could only carry them like that. And somebody said, this is man there with the, with the nave blue thing, that is Mrs. Picker's son. That was my husband building, building then. Little did I know that we will end up together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met him there, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him there. It wasn't love at first, but it just grew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and tell me about the marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: The marriage--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So tell me--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We returned home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leave Russia then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the whole trip on a piece of paper. I had-- my mother, she was in this art studio, had a little block. And this block, this, this was something was out of this world. Do I have it here? Do you, do you want me to, to show it to you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this book, I wrote every station, every day, every hour where we arrived with this train back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did it come about that you were able to leave Russia, because it was after the war had finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some people didn't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how did that come about? You were still there in '47.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Most of us were there in '47 still, only-- only 14 people left or 15 or something like that. And the others stayed [INAUDIBLE] Russia. Then we made the hunger strike, because we said, we are Jewish people. And we don't, we don't eat anymore. We have to go home and this and that. And blah, blah, blah. And they sent somebody out to-- we didn't take any, any food anymore. And they sent some VIP out to investigate. And after that, we were released. We worked two, two, two whole years after the war-- 12 hours a day, seven days a week. There were no holidays, nothing. And sometimes, we had to go out at night when they said, for instance, we have to go straight away to this field to get the potatoes in. Because a frost was forecast. So we had to go out. And I remember once, we had to go out. That was not in this camp. And it was so dark, we only had to feel what grew there so we knew what it was. And the, the potato field was already done. The potatoes were in a heap. And we had to go in with, with a cart and put the potatoes in. And then we couldn't take the cart out, because it sank into the soft soil. Things happened-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop there.
